perturbation.original_text: Excite@Home will respond to change requests within seven (7) days of receipt, and any failure to so respond shall be deemed an approval.
perturbation.changed_text: Excite@Home will respond to change requests within one (1) day of receipt, and any failure to so respond shall be deemed an approval.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the response time to 1 day may be an unreasonable expectation for Excite@Home. California law requires that contract terms must not be unconscionable.
perturbation.law_explanation: California law states that if a contract is unconscionable at the time it was made, the court may refuse to enforce the contract, or it may enforce the remainder of the contract without the unconscionable clause, or it may so limit the application of any unconscionable clause as to avoid any unconscionable result. Demanding response in 1 day for change requests might be deemed unconscionable.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1670.5 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 4. UNLAWFUL CONTRACTS [1667 - 1670.12] ( Title 4 enacted 1872. ) 1670.5. (a) If the court as a matter of law finds the contract or any clause of the contract to have been unconscionable at the time it was made the court may refuse to enforce the contract, or it may enforce the remainder of the contract without the unconscionable clause, or it may so limit the application of any unconscionable clause as to avoid any unconscionable result. (b) When it is claimed or appears to the court that the contract or any clause thereof may be unconscionable the parties shall be afforded a reasonable opportunity to present evidence as to its commercial setting, purpose, and effect to aid the court in making the determination. (Added by Stats. 1979, Ch. 819.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the response time for change requests from seven days to one day. The perturbation explanation suggests this might be an unreasonable expectation, and the law explanation mentions that demanding a response in one day might be deemed unconscionable under California law. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing California Civil Code 1670.5(a), states that if a court finds a contract or clause to be unconscionable, it may refuse to enforce the contract or the unconscionable clause. Therefore, reducing the response time to one day, if deemed unconscionable, could render the clause unenforceable, creating a legal contradiction. The 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' definition further clarifies that a contradiction arises when a contract sets a deadline that violates federal or state law, making the terms unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: Within three business days of receiving Application Provider's written update, Excite@Home will remove any advertising from Application Provider's listed competitors displayed on the Co-Branded Pages.
perturbation.changed_text: Within ten business days of receiving Application Provider's written update, Excite@Home will remove any advertising from Application Provider's listed competitors displayed on the Co-Branded Pages.
perturbation.explanation: Increasing the time frame to 10 business days may violate California's Unfair Competition Law if the delay causes demonstrable harm. This is because competitors could face unfair disadvantage during that extended period.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 17200
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Section 17200 prohibits unfair competition. Unreasonably delaying the removal of a competitor's advertisement could be considered an unfair business practice if that delay demonstrably benefits Excite@Home or harms the Application Provider's listed competitors.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 17200 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 5. Enforcement [17200 - 17210] ( Chapter 5 added by Stats. 1977, Ch. 299. ) 17200. As used in this chapter, unfair competition shall mean and include any unlawful, unfair or fraudulent business act or practice and unfair, deceptive, untrue or misleading advertising and any act prohibited by Chapter 1 (commencing with Section 17500) of Part 3 of Division 7 of the Business and Professions Code. (Amended by Stats. 1992, Ch. 430, Sec. 2. Effective January 1, 1993.)
perturbation.justification: The modification increases the time frame for removing competitor ads from 3 to 10 days. The Law Explanation states this delay could violate California Business and Professions Code Section 17200 if it demonstrably benefits Excite@Home or harms the Application Provider's competitors. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines unfair competition as any unlawful, unfair, or fraudulent business act or practice. The Perturbation Explanation says this could create an unfair disadvantage during that period, which is a direct violation of BPC 17200 as it harms competitors.
